answer.text: Buying a longer-dated option and selling a shorter-dated option at the same strike — profits from the differential time decay rate between the two options (shorter-dated option decays faster)
Buying options that expire on different days of the week
A spread between options on two different calendar year contracts
The same as a bull call spread but with different expiry dates

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Calendar (horizontal/time) spread: Same strike, different expiries. The near-month option decays faster (higher theta) than the far-month. If the underlying stays near the strike, the near-month expires worthless while the far-month retains value. Net effect: profit from differential time decay. **The Trap:** Calendar spreads use SAME strike, DIFFERENT expiry (horizontal). Vertical spreads use DIFFERENT strikes, SAME expiry. It's not about week days or calendar years. **Cross-Exam Context:** Calendar spreads work in equity (VIII), currency, and commodity options. They're a key tool for trading the term structure of implied volatility. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Calendar spreads profit when the underlying stays near the strike — they're an alternative to iron condors for neutral strategies. They also benefit from IV expansion in the back month.
